actions.runtimeAccessDenied: Windows denied access while Claw Studio was finalizing the bundled runtime files. Read the desktop main log and gateway log, then close stale OpenClaw or Claw Studio processes and release antivirus or indexer locks before retrying.
actions.checkLogsAndRetry: Read the gateway log and desktop main log to identify the last activation stage and supervisor error before retrying the bundled runtime.

openclaw.recommendedReason: OpenClaw docs recommend onboarding-first setup, and Windows hosts are usually most stable through WSL2.
openclaw.firstRun: Finish provider setup, choose channels, and add starter skills before your first live workflow.

prerequisites.one: Node 24 is recommended. Node 22.16+ is still supported.
prerequisites.two: Windows should prefer WSL2 for the cleanest day-to-day runtime.
prerequisites.three: Docker is optional for isolated gateways, not the default install path.

zeroclaw.recommendedPath: Clone the repo and install with Cargo.
zeroclaw.recommendedReason: The install manifests and vendor docs both use the Rust source profile; the old pnpm alias is only a compatibility shim.
zeroclaw.firstRun: Confirm `zeroclaw --version` works, then continue with your own provider, channel, and hardware tuning.

prerequisites.one: Install Git plus the Rust toolchain with Cargo.
prerequisites.two: Windows builds should have Visual Studio Build Tools or an equivalent C++ toolchain.
prerequisites.three: ZeroClaw is optimized for tiny memory budgets and low-cost hardware.

ironclaw.recommendedPath: Clone the repo, build with Cargo, then run onboarding.
ironclaw.recommendedReason: IronClaw is source-first today and its README explicitly requires PostgreSQL + pgvector plus NEAR AI authentication.
ironclaw.firstRun: Create the database, enable pgvector, then run `ironclaw onboard` to finish auth and secret setup.

prerequisites.one: Install Git, Rust, and Cargo before building.
prerequisites.two: Prepare PostgreSQL + pgvector for memory and search features.
prerequisites.three: Keep NEAR AI credentials ready for the setup wizard.

orchestrator.name: Orchestrator
orchestrator.summary: Coordinate multiple specialists, decompose tasks, and keep delivery aligned.
orchestrator.description: A control-plane agent for routing work, checking progress, and deciding when to parallelize versus execute locally.
orchestrator.focus: Keeps multi-agent execution disciplined and traceable.

coding-engineer.name: Coding Engineer
coding-engineer.summary: Implement features, debug code, and work through bounded engineering tasks.
coding-engineer.description: Optimized for code changes, small refactors, test repairs, and pragmatic delivery inside existing repositories.
coding-engineer.focus: Prefers concrete patches and defensible engineering tradeoffs.

ops-responder.name: Ops Responder
ops-responder.summary: Handle incidents, triage runtime failures, and drive operational recovery.
ops-responder.description: Designed for incident response, runbook execution, rollback decisions, and controlled mitigation under pressure.
ops-responder.focus: Fast diagnosis, explicit evidence, and low-risk containment.

research-analyst.summary: Collect evidence, compare sources, and turn findings into structured decisions.

content-writer.name: Content Writer
content-writer.summary: Draft launch copy, product documentation, and polished stakeholder messaging.
content-writer.description: Useful for docs, announcements, product narratives, and editing work that needs structure and consistency.

support-triage.name: Support Triage

support-triage.description: Built for frontline support intake, ticket hygiene, and user-facing troubleshooting workflows.
support-triage.focus: Reduce ambiguity quickly and keep support flows actionable.

editor.description: Define a local proxy route, choose the exposed client protocol, point it at an upstream protocol, and keep the projected model defaults together with runtime parameters.
editor.sidebarDescription: Pick a provider on the left to load its preset and recommended model pack.
editor.sidebarSearchPlaceholder: Search provider, vendor, or model family
editor.customRouteHint: Keep the form fully manual when you need your own provider id or upstream target.
editor.emptyProviderSearch: No provider matches the current search.
editor.selectedProviderHint: Manual routes stay flexible. Fill in the provider id, upstream, and model catalog exactly the way you want.
editor.accessDescription: Define the route name, credentials, endpoint, and protocol mapping together.
editor.name: Config Name
editor.providerIdHint: Known provider selections fill the canonical provider id here. Override it only when you need a custom route id.
editor.modelsSectionDescription: Append models one row at a time, reorder them, and keep route roles aligned with the list.
editor.modelListEmptyDescription: Start with one model row, then assign default, reasoning, and embedding roles from the right.
editor.selectionDescription: Choose which model the route uses for default chat, reasoning, and embedding tasks.
editor.modelsHint: One model per line. Use `model-id=Display Name`. If this list is empty, the default/reasoning/embedding model ids above will be added automatically.
editor.enabledHint: Disabled routes stay in storage but are not projected into active runtime use.
editor.defaultRouteHint: Only one route per client protocol can remain the default active route.
editor.runtimeDescription: Tune the projected runtime behavior without leaving the provider editor.
editor.streamingHint: Enable streaming responses for the projected route runtime.
editor.requestOverridesDescription: Author raw JSON5 request metadata for headers, auth, proxy, and TLS handling that must survive the local proxy projection pipeline.
editor.requestOverridesHint: Use JSON5. Supported top-level keys are `headers`, `auth`, `proxy`, and `tls`.

apply.description: Apply the selected route config to an OpenClaw instance default provider selection and optionally update agent model bindings while the full local-proxy projection rolls out.
apply.agentHint: Checked agents will switch their primary model to the selected LLM, with the reasoning model added as fallback when configured.
